q: What is Snowflake Cortex and why do teams pick it over calling an external LLM API?
a: Cortex is Snowflake's serverless AI layer that exposes LLMs, embeddings, retrieval, and classic ML as SQL functions and managed services that run inside your Snowflake account. Because inference runs within the governance boundary, your data never egresses to a third-party API, existing RBAC and masking policies still apply, and there is no infrastructure to provision. Snowflake also commits that customer data and prompts are not used to train the underlying models. The tradeoff is you're limited to the models and regions Snowflake offers, but for governed enterprise data that's usually the point.
fj: Cortex is just a wrapper that forwards my rows to OpenAI.
fs: Inference runs inside Snowflake's boundary with no data egress, governed by the same RBAC and masking. Snowflake also doesn't train models on your data — that's the whole governance pitch.

followups.q: If inference 'runs inside Snowflake', does that mean a model like Claude or GPT physically runs on Snowflake hardware?
followups.a: Not necessarily on your warehouse compute, but it runs within Snowflake's security and governance perimeter, not by shipping rows to a public vendor endpoint. Some models are hosted by Snowflake directly; for partner models, Snowflake operates the inference inside its boundary so data isn't sent to the vendor's own API. The key promise is no egress to a third-party account and no training on your data.

followups.q: What's the downside of being limited to Snowflake's model menu?
followups.a: You can't instantly adopt a brand-new model the day it launches, and you may lack a niche or self-hosted fine-tune you'd run elsewhere. Region availability also varies, so a model you want may not exist in your region without enabling cross-region inference. For most governed enterprise workloads the curated, vetted menu is a feature, not a bug — but it's a real constraint if you need bleeding-edge or exotic models.

followups.q: How would you prove to an auditor that prompts aren't being used to train the models?
followups.a: Point to Snowflake's contractual and documentation commitments that customer data and prompts are not retained for or used in foundation-model training, plus the fact that inference runs in-account under your existing RBAC, masking, and row-access policies. You can also restrict the `SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX_USER` role and show ACCOUNT_USAGE query/usage history as an audit trail of exactly which functions touched which data.

q: Name the AISQL functions you'd reach for and what each one does.
a: The AISQL family (the AI_ prefixed functions, mostly GA as of Nov 2025) covers the common tasks: AI_COMPLETE for free-form LLM generation, AI_CLASSIFY for single- or multi-label categorization, AI_FILTER for a yes/no predicate you can put in WHERE or JOIN, and AI_AGG to reason across many rows without hitting a context-window limit. For vectors and retrieval there's AI_EMBED and AI_SIMILARITY, and for unstructured inputs there's AI_PARSE_DOCUMENT for PDFs/docs and AI_TRANSCRIBE for audio and video. AI_EXTRACT and AI_SENTIMENT round out structured extraction and tone scoring. They're all called inline in a SELECT, so you build AI pipelines in plain SQL.
fj: I'd write a Python UDF and loop the rows through an LLM client myself.
fs: There's a purpose-built function for each task — AI_FILTER for predicates, AI_AGG for cross-row reasoning, AI_CLASSIFY for labels. Hand-rolling a UDF loop loses pushdown, batching, and the governance story.

followups.q: The older functions were under SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX. How do the AI_ functions relate to those?
followups.a: The `AI_`-prefixed family is the newer, consolidated AISQL surface that supersedes much of the older `SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX.*` set — e.g. `AI_SENTIMENT` over `SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX.SENTIMENT`, `AI_COMPLETE` over `COMPLETE`. The AI_ versions are top-level, often add multimodal and structured-output support, and are the ones to standardize on. The legacy names still work but are the older generation.

followups.q: Why does calling these inline in SQL matter versus a Python loop — what do you actually gain?
followups.a: Inline AISQL participates in the query plan, so you get predicate pushdown, set-based parallelism across the warehouse, and composition with normal SQL (filter cheap first, then call the LLM). A Python UDF loop serializes row-by-row, bypasses the optimizer, and loses the governance/billing visibility you get from native functions. You also keep everything in one transactional SQL statement.

followups.q: What does AI_TRANSCRIBE actually take as input and where does the audio live?
followups.a: `AI_TRANSCRIBE` operates on file references — typically audio/video staged in Snowflake and passed via `TO_FILE`/a FILE value from a stage — and returns the transcript text. Because it's a SQL function over staged files, you can transcribe a whole directory of recordings set-based and then chain `AI_SENTIMENT` or `AI_CLASSIFY` on the output, all without a separate speech-to-text service.

q: When do you use AI_COMPLETE versus a task-specific function like SUMMARIZE, SENTIMENT, TRANSLATE, or EXTRACT_ANSWER?
a: Task-specific functions (the SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX.SUMMARIZE / SENTIMENT / TRANSLATE / EXTRACT_ANSWER family) are tuned for one job, need no prompt engineering, and are cheaper and more predictable for that job — reach for them when the task fits exactly. AI_COMPLETE is the general escape hatch: you pick a model and write your own prompt, which you want for anything bespoke, multi-step, or that needs a custom output shape. A senior move is to prefer the narrow function when it fits and only drop to AI_COMPLETE when you genuinely need the flexibility, since the narrow ones are easier to keep correct and cost less. Newer AI_-prefixed versions (AI_SENTIMENT, AI_TRANSLATE, AI_SUMMARIZE_AGG) supersede some of the older ones.
fj: I just use AI_COMPLETE with a clever prompt for everything.
fs: If the task is exactly summarize/translate/sentiment, the dedicated function is cheaper, needs no prompt tuning, and is more stable. I only reach for AI_COMPLETE when I need a custom prompt or output shape.

followups.q: AI_SENTIMENT differs from the old SENTIMENT function how — what's the practical upgrade?
followups.a: `AI_SENTIMENT` adds entity-aware/aspect-based sentiment: instead of one overall score per text, it can return sentiment per specified category or entity (e.g. food vs service in a review). The legacy `SENTIMENT` returns a single −1..1 overall score. So when you need to know sentiment about a specific aspect, `AI_SENTIMENT` is the upgrade; for a single overall tone the difference is mostly the newer surface.

followups.q: Cost-wise, why is SUMMARIZE typically cheaper than AI_COMPLETE doing the same thing?
followups.a: The dedicated function maps to a specifically chosen, often smaller model and a fixed internal prompt tuned for the task, so you're not paying frontier-model rates or sending extra prompt tokens. With `AI_COMPLETE` you choose the model and write the prompt, so it's easy to over-spend on a bigger model and longer instructions to replicate what the narrow function already does cheaply and predictably.

followups.q: EXTRACT_ANSWER versus AI_COMPLETE with a structured output schema — which for pulling fields from text?
followups.a: `EXTRACT_ANSWER` (and `AI_EXTRACT`) is the quick path for 'find the answer to this question in this text' with no prompt engineering. But when you need multiple typed fields returned as guaranteed-valid JSON, `AI_COMPLETE` with a `response_format` schema is stronger because it constrains every field's shape and type. So: single quick lookups → `EXTRACT_ANSWER`/`AI_EXTRACT`; a strict multi-field record → structured-output `AI_COMPLETE`.

q: Show me how AI_FILTER changes a query, and why it's better than wrapping AI_COMPLETE.
a: AI_FILTER takes a natural-language predicate and returns a BOOLEAN, so you can drop it straight into a WHERE clause (or even a JOIN condition) and let the optimizer treat it like any other filter. That's cleaner and more composable than calling AI_COMPLETE to emit 'yes'/'no' and then string-matching the output, which is brittle and forces you to parse free text. The senior caveat is cost: an LLM predicate runs per row, so you filter cheaply with normal SQL first and only let AI_FILTER see the already-narrowed set. Pairing it with the PROMPT() helper lets you template column values safely into the predicate.
fj: I call AI_COMPLETE asking 'reply YES or NO' and filter on the text.
fs: AI_FILTER returns a real BOOLEAN usable in WHERE/JOIN — no fragile string parsing. And I pre-filter with cheap SQL first so the LLM only scores the rows that survive, since it bills per row.

followups.q: Can the optimizer reorder predicates so the cheap WHERE actually runs before AI_FILTER, or do you have to force it?
followups.a: You shouldn't rely on it implicitly. SQL is declarative and the planner may not guarantee evaluation order across a conjunction, so the safe pattern is to structure the query so the narrow set is materialized first — a CTE/subquery that applies the cheap deterministic filters, then `AI_FILTER` on that result. That makes the 'filter cheap, then LLM' discipline explicit instead of hoping the optimizer short-circuits.

followups.q: How is AI_FILTER in a JOIN condition different from a regular join, and what's the risk?
followups.a: Used in a JOIN, `AI_FILTER` evaluates a natural-language predicate across paired rows — useful for fuzzy/semantic matching that exact keys can't express. The risk is combinatorial blow-up: an LLM call per candidate row pair means a near-Cartesian join can explode into millions of LLM invocations. You constrain the join with cheap equality/range predicates first so `AI_FILTER` only judges a small candidate set.

followups.q: Why use PROMPT() instead of string concatenation to build the predicate?
followups.a: `PROMPT()` is a typed templating helper that safely interpolates column values into a placeholder (`{0}`, `{1}`) rather than concatenating raw text, which keeps the prompt well-formed and avoids quoting/escaping bugs and prompt-injection-style breakage from messy data. It's also clearer and reusable. Plain `||` concatenation is fragile the moment a value contains quotes, braces, or instruction-like text.

q: How does AI_AGG differ from running AI_COMPLETE row by row, and when does it matter?
a: AI_AGG is an aggregate function: it takes a column and a prompt and reasons across all the rows in a group at once, returning a single synthesized answer like a themed summary of every review for a product. Unlike calling AI_COMPLETE per row and then trying to merge outputs, AI_AGG is designed not to be capped by a single model's context window — it handles the fan-in internally. Use it for 'summarize/what are the themes across these N rows' questions where row-by-row would either miss cross-row patterns or overflow context. It pairs naturally with GROUP BY to produce one insight per segment.
fj: I'd AI_COMPLETE each row then concatenate the summaries.
fs: AI_AGG reasons across the whole group in one shot and isn't bounded by the context window. Concatenating per-row summaries loses cross-row themes and can blow past context limits.

followups.q: How does AI_AGG get past the context window if a group has more text than the model can hold?
followups.a: It does the fan-in internally with a hierarchical/map-reduce-style strategy: it partitions the group's rows, summarizes chunks, then combines those partial results, so no single call has to fit every row in one context window. You don't manage the batching. The practical implication is that ordering across rows isn't guaranteed to be globally preserved, so it's best for theme/aggregate questions rather than tasks needing strict sequence.

followups.q: When is AI_AGG the wrong tool even though you're aggregating many rows?
followups.a: When you need exact, deterministic numbers — counts, sums, top-N by a metric. That's a job for normal SQL aggregates, which are precise and free of LLM cost and variance. `AI_AGG` is for qualitative synthesis ('what are the recurring complaints'), not for computing figures, where an LLM can miscount or hallucinate. Route quantitative aggregation to GROUP BY/SUM, qualitative to `AI_AGG`.

followups.q: What's the difference between AI_AGG and AI_SUMMARIZE_AGG?
followups.a: `AI_SUMMARIZE_AGG` is the specialized aggregate for the common 'summarize all these rows' case — no prompt needed, tuned for summarization across a group. `AI_AGG` is the general cross-row reasoning aggregate where you supply your own instruction (e.g. 'list the top 3 objections and who raised them'). Use `AI_SUMMARIZE_AGG` when you literally just want a group summary; reach for `AI_AGG` when the cross-row task is bespoke.

q: How do you choose a model in Cortex, and what is cross-region inference?
a: Cortex offers a menu of models — Anthropic Claude, Meta Llama, Mistral, OpenAI, and Snowflake's own — and you pass the model name as a string to AI_COMPLETE. The choice is a quality/cost/latency tradeoff: a large frontier model for hard reasoning, a smaller model for high-volume cheap classification. Models aren't available in every cloud region, so Snowflake offers cross-region inference, controlled by the account-level CORTEX_ENABLED_CROSS_REGION parameter, which lets Cortex route a request to a region where the model exists. The catch is that inference data may transit another region, so for strict data-residency requirements you keep it constrained rather than ANY_REGION.
fj: I always pick the biggest, smartest model to be safe.
fs: Match the model to the job — a cheap small model for bulk classification, a frontier model only for hard reasoning. And cross-region inference has a residency implication: ANY_REGION can route your request out of region, which may not be acceptable.

followups.q: How do you constrain cross-region inference to satisfy data residency instead of just turning it off?
followups.a: Set `CORTEX_ENABLED_CROSS_REGION` to a specific allowed region group rather than `ANY_REGION` — for example a value scoped to your continent/cloud so requests can only route to approved regions where the model exists. Setting it to `DISABLED` blocks cross-region entirely (and you only get models native to your region). The middle setting lets you keep model availability without violating residency.

followups.q: How do you decide empirically which model is 'good enough' for a bulk classification job?
followups.a: Run a small labeled eval set through candidate models and compare accuracy/agreement against the cheapest one, using AI Observability or a simple SQL comparison. Pick the smallest model that clears your accuracy bar, since at high row counts the cost gap between a small and frontier model is often 10x+. You only escalate to a bigger model for the hard subset of rows the small one gets wrong.

followups.q: What happens if you pass a model name that isn't available in your region and cross-region is off?
followups.a: The function errors — the model simply isn't reachable, rather than silently falling back. That's why you either enable (scoped) cross-region inference to reach it or choose a model that's GA in your region. It's worth checking the regional availability matrix when productionizing, because a model in your dev region may not exist in the region you deploy to.

q: What is the semantic model behind Cortex Analyst, and why does it beat raw text-to-SQL?
a: Cortex Analyst is grounded by a semantic model — either a YAML file on a stage or a native semantic view object — that defines the tables, the relationships, the named metrics, and the business synonyms (so 'revenue' maps to the right SUM of the right column). That grounding is what makes it reliable: instead of guessing schema from raw DDL, the model answers in the business's vocabulary and uses pre-defined metric definitions, which is exactly where naive text-to-SQL hallucinates joins and miscomputes aggregates. A semantic view has the advantage of living in Snowflake under RBAC and sharing, versus a YAML you manage on a stage. The semantic layer is the product — the LLM is the easy part.
fj: Cortex Analyst just reads my schema and writes SQL automatically.
fs: It's grounded by a curated semantic model — metrics, relationships, and business synonyms — not raw DDL. That curation is why it beats generic text-to-SQL, which guesses joins and aggregations and quietly gets numbers wrong.

followups.q: What specifically goes wrong if you omit relationship/join definitions from the semantic model?
followups.a: Analyst has to guess how tables connect, and that's exactly where text-to-SQL fails — wrong join keys, fan-out from one-to-many joins double-counting a metric, or picking the wrong path between tables. Declaring relationships pins the join graph so a question that spans tables uses the intended keys and grain, preventing inflated sums. The metric definitions and relationships together are what make aggregates trustworthy.

followups.q: Why define metrics in the semantic model instead of letting Analyst write the SUM itself?
followups.a: A named metric like `revenue = SUM(amount)` with the right filters (e.g. excluding refunds) encodes the one correct business definition, so every question resolves to the same number. If Analyst derives the aggregate ad hoc, two phrasings of 'revenue' can produce two different SQLs and two different totals. Centralizing the metric is what makes results consistent and auditable.

followups.q: How do synonyms actually change Analyst's behavior at query time?
followups.a: Synonyms map business vocabulary to model entities, so when a user says 'GMV' or 'sales' Analyst resolves it to the `revenue` metric instead of failing or guessing a raw column. Without them, colloquial terms either don't match anything or match the wrong column by name similarity. They widen the set of natural phrasings the model answers correctly without changing the underlying SQL.

q: A stakeholder says Cortex Analyst gives wrong numbers sometimes. How do you raise its accuracy?
a: The biggest lever is verified queries: you curate a repository of known-good question/SQL pairs so that when a user asks something similar, Analyst reuses the trusted query instead of generating fresh SQL. Beyond that, you tighten the semantic model — add missing synonyms, clarify ambiguous column names, and define metrics explicitly so it can't pick the wrong column. You also handle ambiguity by having Analyst ask a clarifying question rather than guess, and you constrain it to the curated tables so it can't wander. Snowflake can even analyze your verified queries to auto-enrich the rest of the semantic layer, widening the set of questions it answers correctly.
fj: I'd just switch it to a bigger LLM and hope the SQL improves.
fs: Accuracy comes from the semantic layer, not the model. I add verified queries so common questions reuse trusted SQL, fix ambiguous metric/synonym definitions, and make it ask for clarification instead of guessing.

followups.q: How does a verified query get matched to a new user question — is it exact-match only?
followups.a: No — the Verified Query Repository (VQR) is used by similarity, not exact string match. When a new question is semantically close to a verified one, Analyst leans on that trusted SQL (using the model's logical table/column names) rather than generating from scratch. That's why a modest set of well-chosen verified queries can cover a large cluster of phrasings, and why the queries must reference the semantic model's names to be reusable.

followups.q: What's the newer optimization feature that uses verified queries beyond direct reuse?
followups.a: Snowflake can analyze your verified queries to automatically enrich the rest of the semantic layer — extracting useful definitions, filters, and relationships and folding them in (a Preview feature from late 2025). So verified queries help even for questions that don't match one directly, because the lessons from them improve the semantic model that all questions are grounded on.

followups.q: Why is 'ask a clarifying question' better than letting Analyst pick a reasonable interpretation?
followups.a: A confident wrong answer is worse than a clarifying prompt because the stakeholder can't tell it's wrong — it erodes trust in the whole tool. When a question is ambiguous ('top customers' by revenue or by order count?), having Analyst ask resolves the intent instead of silently choosing, so the returned number is the one the user actually meant. Guessing optimizes for looking smart over being correct.

q: Explain how Cortex Search works under the hood and what TARGET_LAG controls.
a: Cortex Search is a managed retrieval service: you point it at a text column and it builds and maintains a hybrid index combining vector (semantic) and keyword (lexical) search, then applies semantic reranking so the most relevant passages float to the top — that hybrid-plus-rerank stack measurably beats vector-only retrieval. You don't manage chunking infrastructure, embedding refresh, or an external vector DB. TARGET_LAG declares how fresh the index should stay relative to the base table; Snowflake refreshes the service to keep it within that lag as source rows change, trading compute cost against staleness. It serves low-latency queries (hundreds of ms) and is the retrieval engine you ground a RAG chatbot on.
fj: It's just a vector database I have to keep re-embedding myself.
fs: It's hybrid vector+keyword with semantic reranking, and it auto-maintains the index. TARGET_LAG sets the freshness SLA so I don't manage re-embedding or refresh jobs — that's the managed part.

followups.q: Which embedding model powers Cortex Search, and can you bring your own?
followups.a: The vector component is powered by Snowflake's Arctic Embed family (e.g. Arctic Embed M) by default. More recently Cortex Search added Preview support for custom/precomputed vector embeddings, so you can supply vectors from your own or a third-party model when the default doesn't fit your domain — but the managed Arctic default is the zero-config path most teams use.

followups.q: What does setting TARGET_LAG too tight versus too loose cost you?
followups.a: Too tight (e.g. a few minutes) means frequent refreshes and higher serverless compute cost to keep the index near-real-time. Too loose means cheaper refresh but stale results — a user could search for a doc added an hour ago and not find it. You set it to the freshness the use case actually needs: minutes for live support content, hours for a slowly-changing knowledge base.

followups.q: Why does hybrid plus a reranker beat vector-only retrieval — give the mechanism.
followups.a: Vector search captures semantic similarity but can miss exact terms (product codes, error strings, names) that lexical/keyword search nails; combining them recovers both. The reranker then takes the merged candidate set and scores each against the query with a stronger cross-encoder-style model, fixing the ordering. Snowflake's own benchmarks show NDCG@10 climbing from lexical to vector to hybrid to hybrid+reranker — each layer adds relevance.

q: Walk me through building RAG in Snowflake. When would you use Cortex Search vs hand-rolling it?
a: The managed path is Cortex Search: create a search service over your text column, query it for the top passages, then feed those passages plus the question into AI_COMPLETE to generate a grounded answer — Snowflake handles embedding, the hybrid index, and reranking. The DIY path uses primitives: EMBED_TEXT (e.g. EMBED_TEXT_768) to create vectors, store them in a VECTOR column, and retrieve with VECTOR_COSINE_SIMILARITY in a SQL ORDER BY. You hand-roll only when you need full control over chunking, a custom embedding model, or unusual retrieval logic; otherwise Cortex Search wins because it also gives you keyword matching, reranking, and automatic index maintenance you'd otherwise build and operate yourself.
fj: RAG in Snowflake means I always store embeddings and do cosine similarity by hand.
fs: That DIY VECTOR + VECTOR_COSINE_SIMILARITY path works, but Cortex Search adds keyword matching, reranking, and auto-refresh for free. I only hand-roll when I need a custom embedding model or chunking strategy.

code.label: DIY retrieval with vectors
lines: FROM kb ORDER BY s DESC LIMIT 5;

followups.q: In the DIY path, how do you keep the stored embeddings in sync as source documents change?
followups.a: You own the refresh — typically a Stream on the source table feeding a Task that re-embeds changed rows and updates the VECTOR column. That's exactly the maintenance burden Cortex Search removes via `TARGET_LAG`. If you skip it, your index silently drifts stale and retrieval returns outdated chunks. The operational cost of building and monitoring that refresh pipeline is the main reason to prefer the managed service.

followups.q: Why must the query and the documents use the same embedding model in the DIY path?
followups.a: Cosine similarity is only meaningful when both vectors live in the same embedding space. If you embed documents with `e5-base-v2` (768-dim) and the query with a different model, the dimensions and geometry don't correspond, so similarity scores are garbage. You also can't compare across different dimension sizes at all. Same model, same dimension, for both sides — always.

followups.q: What does chunking strategy affect in retrieval quality, and why might you hand-roll it?
followups.a: Chunk size and boundaries control whether a retrieved passage is self-contained and on-topic: too large dilutes the embedding with mixed topics and wastes context; too small loses the surrounding meaning. Domain docs (legal clauses, code, tables) often need custom splitting that respects structure, which is a reason to DIY when the managed default chunking doesn't fit. Good chunking frequently matters more than the model choice.

q: What are Cortex Agents and what problem do they solve over calling Analyst or Search directly?
a: Cortex Agents (GA Nov 2025) are the orchestration layer: given a natural-language request, an agent plans, decides which tools to call, and runs multiple steps — using Cortex Analyst as the tool for structured/SQL questions and Cortex Search as the tool for unstructured document retrieval, plus optional custom tools via stored procedures or UDFs and even a web-search tool. The value is routing and multi-step reasoning: a question like 'compare the contract terms for these two vendors and tie it to their spend' needs both document retrieval and a SQL aggregation, which a single Analyst or Search call can't do alone. The agent decomposes the task, invokes each tool, evaluates intermediate results, and composes a final answer.
fj: An agent is just Cortex Analyst with a chat box on top.
fs: An agent orchestrates multiple tools and steps — it routes structured sub-questions to Analyst and document sub-questions to Search, then composes them. Analyst alone can't retrieve from PDFs, and Search alone can't aggregate a metric.

followups.q: How do you add a custom capability to an agent that Analyst and Search can't provide?
followups.a: You register a custom tool backed by a stored procedure or UDF (or an external function for an outside API), with a description the agent uses to decide when to call it. So if a task needs a pricing-calculation procedure or a call to an internal service, the agent can invoke it as just another tool alongside Analyst and Search. The tool description quality drives whether the agent picks it at the right time.

followups.q: What governs the data an agent can touch — does orchestration bypass RBAC?
followups.a: No. Each tool call still runs under the caller's privileges, so Analyst only sees tables the role can access, Search only the services it's granted, and custom procs run with their own grants. The agent orchestrates, but it can't read anything the underlying role couldn't read directly — governance is enforced at the tool/query layer, not waived by the agent.

followups.q: How does an agent decide it's done versus needs another tool call?
followups.a: It runs a plan-act-observe loop: after each tool result it evaluates whether the accumulated evidence answers the question or whether a gap remains (e.g. it has the contract text but still needs the spend figure), then either calls another tool or composes the final answer. This iterative reasoning over intermediate results is exactly what a single Analyst or Search call can't do — they answer one shot with no follow-up step.

q: You need to pull structured fields out of thousands of scanned PDFs. How do you do it in Cortex?
a: Land the files in a stage and call AI_PARSE_DOCUMENT to turn each document into clean text or markdown — its LAYOUT mode preserves tables, headings, and reading order (and is required for image extraction), while OCR mode is the faster default for plain text-heavy scans. With the structure preserved, you then extract specific fields with AI_EXTRACT or AI_COMPLETE using a structured output schema, or use page_split/page_filter to process only the pages you need. Because it's a SQL function, this fans out across the warehouse and scales to thousands of documents without standing up a separate OCR pipeline. The senior tip is LAYOUT for anything with tables or multi-column layout, OCR when it's flat text and you want speed.
fj: I'd export the PDFs and run a separate OCR/Python service to parse them.
fs: AI_PARSE_DOCUMENT does layout-aware extraction in SQL — LAYOUT mode keeps tables and reading order intact. Then AI_EXTRACT pulls the fields. No separate OCR service, and it scales across the warehouse.

followups.q: Concretely, when is LAYOUT mode worth its higher cost over OCR mode?
followups.a: LAYOUT preserves tables, multi-column flow, headings, and reading order, and is required to extract images — so it's worth it for invoices, forms, financial statements, or anything where a value's meaning depends on its position in a table. For flat, single-column text-heavy scans where you just need the words, OCR mode is faster and cheaper. Using LAYOUT on plain text wastes money; using OCR on a table garbles the structure you needed.

followups.q: How do you process only specific pages of a huge PDF to save cost?
followups.a: Use `page_split`/`page_filter` so `AI_PARSE_DOCUMENT` only parses the pages you care about (e.g. just the summary page of a 200-page report) instead of the whole file. Combined with pre-filtering which documents even need parsing, this keeps token and compute cost down. Parsing everything when you only need page 1 is the same 'don't process more than you must' discipline as filtering rows before an LLM call.

followups.q: Why parse first and then extract, rather than feeding the raw PDF straight to AI_EXTRACT?
followups.a: `AI_PARSE_DOCUMENT` does the heavy OCR/layout work to produce clean, structured text or markdown; feeding that to `AI_EXTRACT` (or `AI_COMPLETE` with a schema) gives the extractor reliable input so fields land in the right place. Skipping the parse and hoping a model reads a raw scan directly is less reliable for tables and multi-column docs. Separating the stages also lets you cache parsed text and re-extract different field sets without re-OCRing.

q: Cortex also has classic ML functions like FORECAST and ANOMALY_DETECTION. When do those beat a GenAI approach?
a: Cortex ML functions — FORECAST, ANOMALY_DETECTION, CLASSIFICATION, and TOP_INSIGHTS — are purpose-built statistical/ML models for numeric and time-series data, trained on your own data in two steps (train a model object, then predict). For 'what will next quarter's demand be', 'which metric points are outliers', or 'which dimension drove this drop', these are the right tool: they're cheaper, deterministic, explainable, and far more accurate on tabular data than asking an LLM to eyeball numbers. GenAI is for language and unstructured content; you don't forecast a revenue series by prompting a chat model. Knowing to route numeric/time-series problems to the ML functions and language problems to AISQL is the senior signal.
fj: I'd ask AI_COMPLETE to predict next month's sales from the history.
fs: Numeric forecasting and outlier detection belong to FORECAST and ANOMALY_DETECTION — purpose-built, cheaper, deterministic, and explainable. LLMs are for language, not for projecting a time series.

followups.q: How does FORECAST handle multiple series, like demand per store, in one model?
followups.a: You provide a series identifier column so a single `FORECAST` model fits and predicts each series (store, SKU) separately within one call, rather than training a model per store by hand. You can also pass exogenous/future regressors (e.g. promotions, holidays) to improve accuracy. So one model object covers a whole panel of time series, and the prediction returns per-series forecasts.

followups.q: Why is 'deterministic and explainable' a real advantage of the ML functions over an LLM here?
followups.a: The ML functions return prediction intervals and, for anomaly/insights, attributions you can defend to a stakeholder ('this point is outside the 99% interval'). They give the same output for the same input, which matters for finance and audit. An LLM eyeballing numbers gives a plausible-sounding figure that varies run to run and can't show its statistical work — unacceptable when a number drives a decision.

followups.q: Where would you still combine the ML functions with GenAI in one pipeline?
followups.a: Use `FORECAST`/`ANOMALY_DETECTION`/`TOP_INSIGHTS` to compute the numbers and surface drivers, then use `AI_COMPLETE` to narrate them — turn 'metric X dropped, driven by region Y' into a readable executive summary. The ML side owns correctness of the figures; the LLM owns the language layer. That division of labor plays to each tool's strength instead of asking the LLM to do arithmetic.

q: When would you fine-tune a model in Cortex versus prompting or RAG?
a: Reach for prompting first and RAG when the model needs current or proprietary facts — those are cheaper, faster to iterate, and don't bake knowledge into weights. Cortex Fine-tuning (serverless, parameter-efficient/PEFT) earns its place when you need to teach a consistent style, format, or domain behavior that prompting can't reliably enforce, or to make a smaller, cheaper model match a larger one's quality on a narrow task. It is not how you inject knowledge — facts go in via RAG, not fine-tuning. You provide labeled training pairs, Snowflake produces an adaptor, and you call it through AI_COMPLETE; cost is per training token plus inference, so you weigh that against just using a bigger base model.
fj: If the model isn't accurate enough, fine-tune it on my documents.
fs: Fine-tuning teaches style and behavior, not facts — facts belong in RAG. I exhaust prompting and retrieval first, and only fine-tune to lock in a format or to make a small cheap model match a big one on a narrow task.

followups.q: Why doesn't fine-tuning reliably inject new facts the way people assume?
followups.a: PEFT fine-tuning adjusts a small adaptor to shift behavior/style, not to memorize a knowledge base, and any facts it does absorb become frozen, hard to update, and prone to confident hallucination when asked beyond the training set. RAG keeps facts in an external, governed, refreshable store the model reads at query time, so you update a document instead of retraining. That's why facts go in via retrieval and fine-tuning shapes form.

followups.q: How do you justify fine-tuning cost over just using a bigger base model?
followups.a: Fine-tuning pays off when a small, cheap model fine-tuned for a narrow high-volume task can match a frontier model's quality there — the per-inference savings at scale outweigh the one-time training token cost. If volume is low, the bigger base model with good prompting is simpler and likely cheaper overall. So the math is: training cost + cheaper inference × volume vs frontier inference × volume.

followups.q: What does the training data for Cortex Fine-tuning look like, and what's a common failure?
followups.a: You supply labeled prompt/completion pairs (typically a Snowflake table/column of inputs and desired outputs) demonstrating the target style or format. The common failure is a small, inconsistent, or unrepresentative dataset — the adaptor overfits to quirks and the model regresses on anything outside it. Quality and consistency of the examples drive the result far more than quantity beyond a threshold.

q: How do you keep Cortex LLM outputs safe and machine-parseable in production?
a: Two distinct controls. For safety, Cortex Guard is a flag on the completion that filters unsafe or harmful model responses — you enable it so the model's output is screened before it reaches users. For reliability of the data shape, use structured outputs: pass a response_format JSON schema (GA) so the model is forced to return JSON conforming to your schema, which removes the brittle 'parse free text and hope' step downstream. Together they mean you can trust both the content and the structure. The PROMPT() helper additionally lets you template column values into prompts cleanly instead of fragile string concatenation.
fj: I parse the model's free-text answer with a regex and add a 'be safe' line to the prompt.
fs: Use the real controls: Cortex Guard to filter unsafe responses, and a response_format JSON schema so output is guaranteed-valid JSON. Regex-parsing free text and 'please be safe' prompts are not production guardrails.

followups.q: What exactly does Cortex Guard do when it catches an unsafe response, and what powers it?
followups.a: Cortex Guard (enabled via the `guardrails: true` option on the completion) screens the model's generated response and, if it falls into harmful categories — violent crime, hate, sexual content, self-harm, and similar — replaces it with a 'Response filtered by Cortex Guard' message instead of returning the unsafe text. It's powered by Meta's Llama Guard. Note it evaluates the model output, so it's an output filter, not a prompt-rewrite.

followups.q: How does response_format guarantee valid JSON rather than just nudging the model?
followups.a: It's constrained/grammar-based decoding: `AI_COMPLETE` validates each generated token against your JSON schema during generation, so the output is forced to conform to the structure and types you declared rather than the model freely emitting text you hope is JSON. That GA mechanism (with `$ref` support for reusable sub-schemas) removes the parse-and-pray step — downstream code can rely on the shape.

followups.q: If both Guard and structured output are on, what happens when Guard filters a response that was supposed to be JSON?
followups.a: Guard takes precedence on safety: a filtered response returns the safety message, not your schema-shaped JSON, so downstream code must handle the 'filtered' case rather than assuming every row yields a valid object. In practice you check for the filtered sentinel before treating the output as parseable JSON. The two controls are independent — structure conformance doesn't override the safety filter.

q: How is Cortex billed, and how do you monitor and control the spend?
a: Cortex AI functions bill in credits per token (input plus output), so cost scales with rows processed, prompt size, and crucially the model you pick — a frontier model can be an order of magnitude more expensive per token than a small one. The danger pattern is an LLM function over a huge unfiltered table, which can quietly run up a large bill in one query. You monitor with the ACCOUNT_USAGE Cortex functions usage history views, which break out tokens and credits per function and model, and you control spend by pre-filtering rows before the AI call, choosing the cheapest model that meets quality, and setting budgets/cost controls Snowflake now provides for AI functions. Cortex Search and Analyst have their own serverless cost components on top.
fj: Cortex is just part of my warehouse cost, so I don't track it separately.
fs: It's per-token credits and very sensitive to model choice and row count — an LLM over an unfiltered table is a runaway-bill trap. I watch the Cortex usage history views and pre-filter rows before the AI call.

code.label: Per-model token/credit usage
lines: GROUP BY 1,2 ORDER BY credits DESC;

followups.q: How is Cortex Search billed differently from the per-token AI functions?
followups.a: Cortex Search has its own serverless cost components: compute to build and refresh the index (driven by data volume and your `TARGET_LAG`), plus storage for the index, plus serving cost for queries — not a simple per-token charge like `AI_COMPLETE`. So a tight `TARGET_LAG` on a large, frequently-changing corpus is a real cost lever distinct from inference tokens. Analyst likewise has its own serverless billing on top of any AI functions it invokes.

followups.q: How do you put a hard ceiling on Cortex spend rather than just monitoring after the fact?
followups.a: Use Snowflake's budgets/cost controls now available for AI functions to cap or alert on Cortex consumption, and pair that with resource monitors on the warehouses involved. Operationally you also gate the runaway pattern at the query level — pre-filter rows and pick the cheapest adequate model — so you never depend solely on a budget catching it after credits are burned.

followups.q: Why does prompt size matter as much as row count for cost?
followups.a: Billing is per input plus output token, so a verbose system prompt or stuffing huge context into every row multiplies input tokens by the row count — a 2x longer prompt on a million rows is a million extra prompt-token charges. Trimming instructions, retrieving only the top-k passages instead of dumping whole documents, and capping output length all directly cut the bill. It's not just how many rows, but how many tokens each row costs.

q: A security reviewer asks how Cortex handles data governance. What's your answer?
a: Inference runs inside your Snowflake account, so data isn't sent to an external model provider and stays within the same security perimeter, subject to your existing RBAC, masking, and row-access policies. Access to Cortex is itself governed by database roles: SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX_USER (granted to PUBLIC by default) gates the AI functions, and you can revoke it from PUBLIC and grant only to approved roles — there are narrower roles like CORTEX_ANALYST_USER for least privilege. Snowflake states customer data and prompts are not used to train the foundation models, and you can opt out of AI features entirely. Model and feature availability is region-dependent, which intersects with data residency when cross-region inference is enabled.
fj: Governance is fine because Snowflake is SOC 2 compliant.
fs: Concretely: data stays in-account under existing RBAC/masking, access is gated by the CORTEX_USER database role (which I can revoke from PUBLIC), and Snowflake doesn't train on customer data. Cross-region inference is the one residency caveat to check.

followups.q: If a column has a masking policy, does Cortex see the real value or the masked one?
followups.a: Because Cortex functions run as normal SQL under the calling role, they see whatever that role would see — so a role that gets the masked value feeds the masked value into the LLM, and a privileged role feeds the clear value. Masking and row-access policies apply at read time before the function, so governance isn't bypassed. The senior implication: control which roles can both unmask and call Cortex.

followups.q: What's the point of CORTEX_ANALYST_USER if CORTEX_USER already exists?
followups.a: It's least privilege: `CORTEX_ANALYST_USER` grants access to Cortex Analyst specifically without handing out the broader `CORTEX_USER` ability to call every AI function. So a BI team that should only ask analytical questions gets Analyst access without the power to run arbitrary `AI_COMPLETE` over sensitive tables. Scoping to the narrowest role that does the job is the governance best practice.

followups.q: How do you give an auditor a record of exactly what data Cortex processed?
followups.a: Use ACCOUNT_USAGE — the Cortex functions usage history shows which functions and models ran with token counts, and standard query history shows the actual SQL (including the tables referenced) per user and role. Together they form the audit trail of who invoked which AI function over which objects. That's a concrete answer beyond 'we're compliant' — it's queryable evidence.

q: How do you evaluate the quality of a Cortex RAG app before you ship it?
a: Use Snowflake's AI Observability, which is powered by TruLens: you instrument the app with tracing, build a test set of representative questions (and ideally reference answers), and run batch evaluations that score each response on metrics like groundedness — is the answer actually supported by the retrieved context — and context relevance — did retrieval pull the right passages. The metrics use an LLM-as-judge (you can pick the judge model; a default like a Llama model is used otherwise) to score 0–1 with an explanation, so you can isolate whether failures are a retrieval problem or a generation problem. The point is to measure quality with a repeatable harness instead of eyeballing a few demo queries before going live.
fj: I click through a handful of questions, and if they look right we ship.
fs: I run AI Observability / TruLens evals on a test set — groundedness and context relevance scored by an LLM judge — so I can tell whether failures come from retrieval or generation. Vibe-checking a few queries isn't evaluation.

followups.q: A low groundedness but high context-relevance score tells you what?
followups.a: Retrieval is working — the right passages were pulled (high context relevance) — but generation is the problem: the model isn't sticking to the retrieved context and is adding unsupported claims (low groundedness). The fix is on the generation side: tighten the prompt to answer only from context, lower temperature, or switch the generation model — not the retriever. Splitting the metrics is exactly what lets you localize the fault.

followups.q: Why use an LLM-as-judge instead of exact-match against reference answers?
followups.a: RAG answers are free-form, so a correct response can be phrased a hundred ways that exact string match would mark wrong. An LLM judge scores semantic properties — is this grounded in the context, is it relevant — and returns a 0–1 score with an explanation you can inspect. The tradeoff is judge variability, which you mitigate by fixing the judge model and reviewing its rationales rather than trusting the number blindly.

followups.q: What does instrumenting with tracing buy you beyond the final score?
followups.a: Tracing captures the intermediate steps — the retrieved passages, the prompt sent, tool calls, latencies — so when a score is bad you can see exactly which stage failed instead of only the end answer. For a multi-step agent or RAG chain that's essential to debugging, because the failure could be retrieval, reranking, prompt assembly, or generation. The score tells you something's wrong; the trace tells you where.

q: Design a chatbot over Snowflake that answers both 'what's revenue this quarter' and 'what does the contract say'. How do you route?
a: Structured, metric-style questions go to Cortex Analyst (grounded by the semantic model, it generates and runs SQL), and unstructured 'what does this document say' questions go to Cortex Search for passage retrieval feeding AI_COMPLETE. The clean way to do the routing is a Cortex Agent, which decides per turn which tool to invoke — or which to invoke for each part of a compound question — rather than you writing brittle if/else intent logic. For the UI, Streamlit-in-Snowflake keeps the whole app inside the governance boundary with no data leaving the account. The mental model: structured numbers to Analyst, documents to Search, an Agent to route and combine, Streamlit to serve.
fj: I'll send every question to Cortex Analyst and let it figure it out.
fs: Analyst only does structured SQL — it can't read a contract PDF. I route metric questions to Analyst and document questions to Search, and let a Cortex Agent decide per question. Streamlit-in-Snowflake keeps it all in-boundary.

followups.q: For the compound question 'tie contract terms to spend', how does the agent actually combine the two tools?
followups.a: The agent decomposes it: it calls Cortex Search to retrieve the relevant contract clauses and Cortex Analyst to compute the spend figure, then in its reasoning loop joins the two intermediate results into one answer ('the renewal clause caps at $X; actual spend was $Y'). Neither tool alone can do both halves, and the agent's plan-act-observe loop is what stitches the document evidence to the numeric result.

followups.q: Why Streamlit-in-Snowflake instead of an external web app calling Cortex via REST?
followups.a: Streamlit-in-Snowflake runs the app inside the account, so data and prompts never leave the governance boundary and the app inherits Snowflake auth/RBAC with no separate data egress to secure. An external app over the REST API works but reintroduces a perimeter to lock down — auth, network, and the question of data leaving the account. For a governed enterprise chatbot, keeping it in-boundary is the simpler, safer default.

followups.q: How do you stop the agent from answering a 'revenue' question out of a stale document instead of live SQL?
followups.a: Tool descriptions and routing drive this: Analyst is described as the source of truth for metrics/numbers and Search for document text, so the agent routes a quantitative question to Analyst's live SQL rather than a possibly-outdated PDF. You reinforce it by scoping each tool's domain clearly and, for numbers, relying on the semantic model's verified metrics. Sloppy tool descriptions are what cause mis-routing.

q: Your AI_COMPLETE call over a 200M-row table failed partway and you got a partial, expensive result. What went wrong and how do you fix it?
a: Running an LLM function across a massive unfiltered table is both slow and a cost risk, and a single hard error on one row can abort the whole statement, leaving you billed for the tokens already processed. The fixes: pre-filter aggressively so the LLM only sees rows that actually need it, materialize results incrementally (batch by partition or process via a stream/task so a failure resumes instead of restarting), and use the TRY_ variant / error-detail handling so a bad row returns an error value rather than killing the query. Pick a cheaper model for bulk passes and reserve the frontier model for the hard subset. This is the same 'filter cheap, then AI' discipline that controls cost.
fj: I'll just rerun the same query on a bigger warehouse so it finishes.
fs: A bigger warehouse doesn't fix it — the issues are an unfiltered scan and one bad row aborting the statement. I pre-filter, batch incrementally so it resumes, and use TRY_COMPLETE / error handling so a single row can't kill (and re-bill) the whole run.

code.label: Resilient bulk AI
lines: -- only rows that need an LLM

followups.q: Exactly how does TRY_COMPLETE behave on a row that COMPLETE would error on?
followups.a: `TRY_COMPLETE` returns `NULL` (rather than raising) when the underlying completion would error on that row, so one bad row no longer aborts the whole statement and you keep the good results. You then find the `NULL`s and retry or handle them separately. Plain `COMPLETE` raising on a single row is what wastes the tokens already processed by killing the run — the `TRY_` variant is the resilience knob.

followups.q: Why does a Stream make the job resumable where a plain SELECT doesn't?
followups.a: A Stream tracks the unconsumed change rows; once a Task processes them within a transaction, the offset advances so already-handled rows aren't reprocessed. If a run fails, the next run picks up only the remaining rows instead of rescanning (and re-billing) the whole 200M-row table. A bare `INSERT ... SELECT` over the full table has no offset, so a failure means starting over from scratch.

followups.q: How do you split work so the frontier model only sees the hard subset?
followups.a: Run a cheap small model (or a deterministic rule) over everything first to get a confidence signal or a coarse label, then route only the low-confidence/ambiguous rows to the frontier model in a second pass. Most rows resolve cheaply; the expensive model is reserved for the few that need it. This two-tier cascade controls cost while keeping accuracy on the hard cases — the same filter-cheap-then-AI discipline applied to model selection.

q: Compare AI_CLASSIFY with the Cortex ML CLASSIFICATION function. They sound the same.
a: They solve different problems. AI_CLASSIFY is a zero-shot LLM call: you give it plain-language category definitions and it labels free text or images with no training data, ideal when labels are described in words (topic, intent, sentiment bucket) and you have no labeled history. The ML CLASSIFICATION function trains a supervised model on your own labeled tabular features and predicts a class from numeric/categorical columns — it needs training data but is cheaper at inference, deterministic, and stronger on structured signals. So: unstructured text/no labels and semantic categories → AI_CLASSIFY; you have labeled tabular features and want a fast, repeatable predictor → ML CLASSIFICATION. Choosing wrong means either paying LLM rates for a tabular problem or having no training data for the supervised one.
fj: Both classify rows, so I'd just use whichever I've heard of.
fs: AI_CLASSIFY is zero-shot over text/images from plain-language labels; ML CLASSIFICATION is a supervised model trained on labeled tabular features. Text with no labels → AI_CLASSIFY. Labeled numeric features → ML CLASSIFICATION, which is cheaper and deterministic.

followups.q: If you have a few thousand labeled text examples, which do you pick?
followups.a: It depends on the signal. If the labels hinge on semantic understanding of language, `AI_CLASSIFY` zero-shot (optionally with a few examples/clear category descriptions) may already nail it without training and stay robust to new phrasings. If the text reduces to extractable features or you want a cheap deterministic high-volume predictor, training ML `CLASSIFICATION` on those examples pays off at scale. Often you prototype with `AI_CLASSIFY`, then graduate to a trained model once volume justifies it.

followups.q: How does AI_CLASSIFY handle multi-label and category descriptions?
followups.a: `AI_CLASSIFY` supports single- and multi-label classification, and you can pass not just label names but descriptions to disambiguate categories, plus task instructions. That lets you steer it ('billing = payment/refund issues, not feature requests') without training data, which is its core advantage over a supervised model that can only learn from labeled rows. Good category descriptions are the main accuracy lever for it.

followups.q: Why is the ML CLASSIFICATION function 'deterministic' in a way AI_CLASSIFY isn't?
followups.a: ML `CLASSIFICATION` is a fixed trained model: the same feature vector produces the same class prediction every run, which matters for reproducibility and audit. `AI_CLASSIFY` is an LLM call whose output can vary slightly and depends on the model version and phrasing. For regulated or repeatability-critical pipelines that determinism plus lower per-inference cost is decisive for the supervised model.

q: AI_COMPLETE supports a system prompt and conversation history. How do you use that for a production multi-turn Cortex chatbot?
a: AI_COMPLETE accepts an array-of-objects payload (the 'messages' form) where each entry has a role ('system', 'user', 'assistant') and content. The system message sets the persona and constraints once; prior turns are replayed as user/assistant pairs so the model has context without you managing state in weights. For a production chatbot you typically store the turn history in a Snowflake table, fetch the relevant window (last N turns or a summary), and assemble the messages array before the call. The senior discipline is to cap the history window — longer conversations push up token cost per call — and to place guardrails in the system prompt rather than leaking them into the user message where they can be overridden by clever inputs.
fj: I just concatenate the whole conversation into one big prompt string.
fs: The messages-array form with explicit role separation is how you do multi-turn cleanly — system/user/assistant roles, history stored in a table, windowed to cap tokens. String concatenation loses structure and makes the system prompt overridable.

code.label: Multi-turn messages payload
lines: [{'role':'system','content':'You are a billing assistant. Answer only from context.'},

followups.q: What happens if the conversation history exceeds the model's context window?
followups.a: The call errors or truncates, and older context is lost either way. The fix is a sliding window: keep only the last K turns, or summarize earlier turns with AI_COMPLETE into a condensed context object stored alongside the history, then replay only the summary plus recent turns. You design the window strategy before hitting limits, not after — uncapped history is a cost and reliability bomb as threads grow.

followups.q: Why put constraints in the system prompt rather than the user message?
followups.a: Most models treat the system message as a privileged instruction layer that user input shouldn't override. If you embed 'answer only from context' in the user turn, a crafted user message can contradict or dilute it. The system prompt is the right boundary layer; keep it owner-authored and static so adversarial user inputs can't redefine the assistant's ground rules.

followups.q: How do you store and retrieve conversation history efficiently in Snowflake for a high-traffic app?
followups.a: Store turns in a table with session_id, turn_seq, role, and content columns. On each request, run a simple `SELECT ... WHERE session_id = ? ORDER BY turn_seq DESC LIMIT N` to get the window, then assemble the messages array in your Streamlit or calling layer. For very active sessions use a semi-structured VARIANT column or a materialized summary row to avoid a wide range scan per turn. The table stays inside the governance boundary, audit-queryable, and doesn't require an external session store.

q: Walk through CORTEX.SENTIMENT versus AI_SENTIMENT — what changed and how do you use each correctly?
a: The original SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX.SENTIMENT returns a single float in [−1, 1] representing the overall tone of an input string — negative, neutral, or positive. No prompt, no configuration. AI_SENTIMENT is the next-generation surface: it adds aspect-based sentiment, letting you specify one or more aspects (e.g., 'delivery', 'packaging', 'price') and get a per-aspect score with evidence snippets instead of one blended number. In production the practical choice is: use SENTIMENT for a simple overall tone signal when you have no aspect structure in mind and cost matters; use AI_SENTIMENT when you need faceted feedback analysis (e.g., a product review split into taste, price, and service). For bulk scoring of millions of records SENTIMENT is also cheaper since it's a purpose-built task model, not a frontier call.
fj: I'd use AI_SENTIMENT for everything since it's the newer one.
fs: For a single overall tone score SENTIMENT is cheaper and sufficient. AI_SENTIMENT earns its cost when I need per-aspect scores — e.g., a review broken down by delivery vs product quality. Use the narrower tool when the narrow output is all you need.

code.label: Aspect-based vs overall sentiment
lines: SELECT SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX.SENTIMENT(review) AS score FROM reviews;

followups.q: What does AI_SENTIMENT actually return — a number or a structured object?
followups.a: AI_SENTIMENT returns a VARIANT object with per-aspect sentiment scores and evidence snippets — the text fragment that drove the score for each aspect — rather than a single float. You access individual aspects with dot notation (`:delivery.score`) and can `LATERAL FLATTEN` the result if you want one row per aspect per review. That's the tradeoff: richer, structured output at higher token cost versus the single float from SENTIMENT.

followups.q: How do you validate that SENTIMENT scores are calibrated for your domain?
followups.a: Sample a stratified set (strongly negative, neutral, positive by human label), run SENTIMENT on it, and compute a confusion matrix or correlation. SENTIMENT is a general model; in a niche domain (medical, legal, financial) 'neutral' language may genuinely skew the score in unexpected ways. If calibration is off, AI_SENTIMENT with explicit aspect definitions or a fine-tuned model is the fix, not just re-prompting SENTIMENT (which takes no prompt).

followups.q: At what row count does the cost difference between SENTIMENT and AI_SENTIMENT become material?
followups.a: It scales linearly — at 10M rows even a small per-token difference becomes significant. SENTIMENT maps to a small, specialized model with minimal input tokens; AI_SENTIMENT invokes a larger model plus aspect context per row. At high volume, prototype on a stratified sample to measure the accuracy uplift, then decide if the aspect granularity is worth the cost multiple. For a million-row table the bill difference can be 5–10× — run the math before committing.

q: How does Cortex Analyst handle ambiguous multi-turn questions, and what's the API contract for clarifying questions?
a: Cortex Analyst is invoked via a REST endpoint (or through the Python SDK / Streamlit) and returns a response type that can be either a SQL+answer or a 'clarification request'. When the semantic model has multiple interpretations for a question — ambiguous metric, conflicting date grain, multiple plausible join paths — Analyst signals this by returning one or more clarifying questions rather than guessing. Your application must check the response type and surface those options to the user; the user picks an interpretation; you send the next message with the user's choice. This is the multi-turn loop. The senior error is treating Analyst as a one-shot REST call and ignoring the clarification response type, which means ambiguous questions silently get a guess that can be wrong and is hard to debug.
fj: I just take Analyst's first SQL response and run it every time.
fs: Analyst can return a clarification-request instead of SQL when the question is ambiguous. My app checks the response type and bounces the options back to the user — ignoring that type means silent wrong answers whenever the question is underdetermined.

code.label: Handle Analyst response type in Python
lines: # surface options to user, then call again with chosen option

followups.q: How do you reduce how often Analyst asks clarifying questions without sacrificing accuracy?
followups.a: Add synonyms and explicit metric definitions to the semantic model so common ambiguous phrasings resolve deterministically rather than branching. Add verified queries for the most-common question patterns so those reuse trusted SQL without needing to reason about ambiguity. The goal is to narrow the underdetermined space in the semantic layer — the clarifying mechanism is a fallback, not a crutch.

followups.q: Can you pre-supply an interpretation to skip clarification for a known question pattern?
followups.a: Yes — verified queries do exactly this: when a new question semantically matches a verified one, Analyst uses that trusted SQL rather than generating fresh SQL or asking for clarification. For frequently asked but previously ambiguous questions, adding a verified query pair is the targeted fix. It's both a quality and UX improvement: users stop being interrupted for questions the semantic model already knows the right answer to.

followups.q: If Analyst generates SQL, what guarantees does the caller have about that SQL — can it harm data?
followups.a: Analyst produces read-only SELECT statements grounded by the semantic model; it won't generate INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, or DDL. The model can only reference tables declared in the semantic model, so scope is constrained at authoring time. Running the SQL still requires the calling role to have SELECT on those tables, so RBAC is the enforcement layer. You should also consider running Analyst-generated SQL through a validation step in regulated environments before executing, since even a SELECT can be expensive.

q: Cortex Search supports column-level attribute filters. How do you use them and why do they matter?
a: When you define a Cortex Search service you can include non-text attribute columns alongside the search column, and at query time you filter on those attributes in the API call to scope results before semantic ranking. For example, a support-doc search service that includes a `product_line` and `region` attribute lets you search 'how to cancel a subscription' restricted to product='Enterprise' and region='EU', so the hybrid retrieval only scores within that slice rather than returning results from other products that happen to be semantically close. Without attribute filtering, semantic similarity alone can surface wrong-product passages that look relevant. This is the difference between a 'global search that ignores context' and a governed retrieval that respects the user's organizational scope.
fj: I'd filter results in Python after retrieval since the search is already fast.
fs: Post-retrieval Python filtering throws away relevance signal — the top-k candidates already excluded the right passages. Attribute filters in the Cortex Search query scope the hybrid index before ranking so you get the top-k within the right slice.

followups.q: How are attribute columns declared when you create the search service?
followups.a: You include them in the `AS SELECT` clause of the `CREATE CORTEX SEARCH SERVICE` statement alongside the text column. Any non-text columns in the SELECT become filterable attributes — the service indexes them for efficient scoped retrieval. They don't participate in the vector/keyword matching, only in pre-ranking filtering. Choosing the right set of attributes at creation time is the design decision; you can't filter on columns you didn't include.

followups.q: What's the consequence of not using attribute filters in a multi-tenant app?
followups.a: Without scoping, a user from Tenant A can retrieve passages indexed from Tenant B's documents if they happen to be semantically similar. This is a data-isolation failure, not just a quality issue. Attribute filters — tenant_id, data_classification, or department — are the enforcement mechanism when multiple tenants share a search service. Alternatively, create separate per-tenant services, but that trades governance simplicity for per-service cost.

followups.q: Can attribute filters combine with a time-range filter for freshness-biased retrieval?
followups.a: Yes — if you include a `created_at` or `updated_at` timestamp column as an attribute, you can add a `@gte`/`@lte` filter to restrict retrieval to recent documents, biasing the index toward fresh content even though Cortex Search's hybrid scoring doesn't inherently rank by date. That's useful for support use cases where a six-month-old KB article may have been superseded by a newer one.

q: You're building a pipeline to ingest earnings call audio and extract financial KPIs. Map out the Cortex functions you'd use.
a: The pipeline has three stages. First, use AI_TRANSCRIBE on the staged audio files to convert speech to text — it accepts audio/video via a TO_FILE reference and returns transcript text, handling multiple speakers and accents. Second, use AI_PARSE_DOCUMENT in LAYOUT mode on any accompanying slide-deck PDFs (charts, tables) so the financial tables are structure-preserved. Third, join the transcript and parsed deck text and run AI_EXTRACT with a schema specifying fields like revenue_guidance, eps_estimate, and growth_rate — or use AI_COMPLETE with a response_format JSON schema for tighter type guarantees. The whole pipeline runs in SQL, fans out in parallel across the warehouse, and never leaves the Snowflake boundary. The senior touch is schema-constraining the extract step so downstream consumers get typed fields, not free text they have to parse.
fj: I'd download the audio files and run a Python speech-to-text script externally first.
fs: AI_TRANSCRIBE handles audio-to-text inside Snowflake, AI_PARSE_DOCUMENT handles the slide deck PDFs with tables intact, and AI_EXTRACT pulls typed KPIs. The whole pipeline runs as SQL over staged files — no external service, no data egress.

followups.q: AI_TRANSCRIBE vs Whisper via a Python UDF — what's the governance trade-off?
followups.a: A Python UDF calling an external Whisper endpoint sends audio outside the Snowflake boundary (or at least outside the account) and bypasses data classification and masking. AI_TRANSCRIBE runs inside the account, governed by the same RBAC as any other function, with no audio egress. For earnings calls or any material non-public information, in-boundary transcription is often non-negotiable from a compliance standpoint, even if an external Whisper model has marginally better accuracy on certain accents.

followups.q: What happens when the earnings call has multiple speakers and AI_TRANSCRIBE can't distinguish them?
followups.a: AI_TRANSCRIBE returns the transcript text without guaranteed diarization (speaker labeling) in all cases — the output is text, not labeled turns. If speaker attribution matters (who said what about guidance), you either accept the unsegmented transcript and use context (the CFO always answers after the analyst question pattern) or run a separate diarization step. For KPI extraction, speaker identity is usually less important than finding the stated figures anywhere in the text.

followups.q: Why use AI_EXTRACT over AI_COMPLETE with a schema for KPI extraction — is there a practical difference?
followups.a: `AI_EXTRACT` is a targeted function for pulling a list of named fields from text with no custom prompt; it's the faster, simpler path when your fields are named and the text is the sole source. `AI_COMPLETE` with `response_format` gives you stricter type control (you define the full JSON schema with types, optionals, and nested structures) and lets you add instructions — e.g., 'if guidance isn't stated, return null rather than inferring'. For simple field lists, AI_EXTRACT is clean; for typed multi-level schemas, the structured-output AI_COMPLETE is more reliable.

q: Walk me through building and deploying a Streamlit-in-Snowflake app that calls Cortex AI functions. What are the key constraints?
a: Streamlit-in-Snowflake (SiS) lets you write and host a Python Streamlit app as a Snowflake object (CREATE STREAMLIT) that runs inside your account — the app and its Cortex calls never leave the boundary, auth is native Snowflake SSO, and you share it like any object. You call Cortex inside the app via the Snowpark session (session.call or session.sql with SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX.*), or via the Python SDK's Cortex functions. Key constraints to know: the app runs in a Snowflake-managed container, so you can't install arbitrary OS packages — only Anaconda-distributed Python packages are available. External network egress is blocked by default; you must create a Network Rule and External Access Integration to reach any outside URL. The app's warehouse provides compute for queries; Cortex serverless functions bill separately. Sharing: grant the STREAMLIT privilege on the object to a role, not file-system access.
fj: I'd just deploy a Streamlit app on a VM and have it call Snowflake via JDBC.
fs: SiS hosts the app in-account so Cortex calls never leave the boundary and Snowflake auth is automatic. The constraints are: Anaconda-only packages, no arbitrary egress without an External Access Integration, and Cortex functions bill separately from the app's warehouse compute.

followups.q: How do you share a Streamlit app with a business analyst who doesn't have a developer role?
followups.a: Grant the `USAGE` privilege on the STREAMLIT object to the analyst's role (and USAGE on the database and schema containing it). The analyst accesses it through Snowsight — no Snowflake client, no development environment. You also ensure the app's warehouse is one their role can use. The app runs under the viewer's own role, so RBAC determines what data it can query — no privilege escalation.

followups.q: What's the External Access Integration needed for and when would you use it in a Cortex app?
followups.a: By default SiS is network-isolated. An External Access Integration plus a Network Rule allows the app to make outbound HTTP calls to a specific host/IP — needed if you want to call a REST webhook, send a Slack notification from the app, or reach a custom API. For a pure Cortex app (all calls go to Snowflake's internal AI functions) you don't need one. You add it only when the app must reach something outside Snowflake.

followups.q: How do you handle secrets (API keys for external calls) inside a Streamlit-in-Snowflake app?
followups.a: Use Snowflake Secrets — create a SECRET object storing the credential, reference it in the External Access Integration, and access it in the app via the `get_generic_secret_string` API rather than hardcoding or using environment variables. The secret is governed by RBAC (who can read it) and never appears in the app code or query history. That's the in-boundary, auditable path versus env vars in app config files.

q: How does Cortex ML ANOMALY_DETECTION work, and how do you operationalize it for continuous monitoring?
a: ANOMALY_DETECTION trains an unsupervised (or supervised, with a labeled anomaly column) model on a time series of numeric values, learns the expected range at each point, and labels new points as anomalies if they fall outside the learned confidence interval. The trained model object is created with CREATE SNOWFLAKE.ML.ANOMALY_DETECTION, then you CALL model!DETECT_ANOMALIES on new data to get each point flagged with a boolean anomaly indicator and an anomaly score. For continuous monitoring you pair it with a Task + Stream: the Stream captures new metric rows as they land, the Task calls DETECT_ANOMALIES on that delta and writes results to a monitoring table, and an Alert fires a notification when anomaly = true. This keeps detection near-real-time without a standing external ML server.
fj: I'd write a Python script that pulls the time series to a notebook and runs a z-score check.
fs: ANOMALY_DETECTION trains an ML model in SQL and runs DETECT_ANOMALIES in a Task+Stream pipeline inside Snowflake — no Python infra, near-real-time, and the model knows the series' baseline better than a static z-score threshold.

code.label: Train then detect in a Task

followups.q: What's the difference between supervised and unsupervised anomaly detection in Cortex ML?
followups.a: Unsupervised (the default) learns normal behavior purely from the historical series — no labels needed. Supervised mode lets you supply a label column marking known anomalies in training, so the model learns specifically what 'bad' looks like for your metric rather than inferring it statistically. Use supervised when you have reliable historical incident records and the unsupervised baseline keeps flagging business-as-usual events (e.g., weekly spikes) as anomalies.

followups.q: How do you handle seasonality so the model doesn't flag every Monday spike as an anomaly?
followups.a: ANOMALY_DETECTION automatically learns seasonal patterns (daily, weekly) if the training series is long enough (several seasonal cycles) — you don't set seasonality manually, it infers it. The implication is that training data must cover enough history: a model trained on two weeks doesn't see the weekly pattern clearly. Provide at least 2–3 full seasonal cycles in training so the learned interval at each seasonal position is right.

followups.q: What's TOP_INSIGHTS and when do you use it versus ANOMALY_DETECTION?
followups.a: `TOP_INSIGHTS` is for root-cause explanation: given a metric that dropped (the 'surprise' input), it scans dimension combinations and identifies which slices account for the change. ANOMALY_DETECTION tells you that something is abnormal; TOP_INSIGHTS tells you which dimension (region + product + channel) drove it. In practice you chain them: detect with ANOMALY_DETECTION, then pass the anomaly window to TOP_INSIGHTS to get an attribution — 'EU + mobile + checkout_error explains 78% of the drop.'

q: Your team wants to fine-tune a Cortex model to output structured incident reports in a specific Markdown template. Walk through the end-to-end PEFT workflow.
a: Start by collecting training pairs: hundreds of (input, desired_output) examples where the output is the exact Markdown incident report format you want — header, timeline, impact, root cause, action items. Quality beats quantity: 300 consistent, high-quality examples outperform 3,000 inconsistent ones. Store them as a Snowflake table with prompt and completion columns. Run CREATE SNOWFLAKE.CORTEX.FINETUNE to start a serverless PEFT training job; you specify the base model (e.g., mistral-7b), the training table, and optionally a validation table. The job runs serverless, no warehouse cost, billed per training token. Once complete, the fine-tuned model appears with a version name you pass to AI_COMPLETE. Before shipping, run an eval: generate outputs on a held-out test set, score structural conformance (does it have all sections, in order), and optionally use AI_COMPLETE or AI_FILTER as an LLM judge for content quality.
fj: I'd just put the template in the system prompt and that's enough.
fs: A system-prompt template works but can drift under adversarial or complex inputs. Fine-tuning locks the format into the model's weights — after 300+ consistent training pairs the output is structurally reliable across inputs, not prompt-dependent. The eval on held-out structural conformance is what proves it.

followups.q: How do you measure whether the fine-tune actually improved on the prompt-only baseline?
followups.a: Run both the base model (with your best system prompt) and the fine-tuned model on the same held-out test set. Score structural conformance programmatically (section headers present, order correct, no extra hallucinated sections) and use an LLM-judge or human review for content quality on a sample. Report pass-rate and quality delta. The fine-tune earns its place only if conformance rate meaningfully improves over the prompt baseline — if the delta is small, the prompt is sufficient and the training cost was wasted.

followups.q: What base models are available for Cortex fine-tuning as of early 2026?
followups.a: As of early 2026, Cortex Fine-tuning supports a curated set of smaller open-weight models — the Mistral 7B family and Llama 3/3.1 variants (e.g., Llama-3.1-8B) — since PEFT is most cost-effective on models where the adaptor cost is justified by cheaper inference versus using a frontier model directly. Frontier models like Claude are not fine-tunable in Cortex. Always check Snowflake's current documentation because the available model list expands with each release.

followups.q: How do you roll back if the fine-tuned model regresses on edge cases after deployment?
